name: Project Sign
description: Project Sign was a U.S. Air Force study of unidentified flying objects (UFOs) initiated in late 1947. It was the first official U.S. Air Force investigation into UFOs. Proponents believe Project Sign collected evidence suggesting the extraterrestrial origin of some UFOs, but that this evidence was suppressed or ignored by higher-ranking officers and subsequent investigations. The project aimed to determine if UFOs posed a threat to national security. It involved analyzing sightings reports from pilots, military personnel, and civilians. Some reports were dismissed as misidentified conventional aircraft, weather phenomena, or psychological illusions. However, a portion of the reports remained unexplained, fueling speculation about advanced technology and potential extraterrestrial origins. Critics argue that Project Sign suffered from methodological flaws, lacked rigorous scientific analysis, and was influenced by Cold War anxieties. Mainstream historical accounts suggest that Project Sign ultimately concluded that there was no conclusive evidence to support the extraterrestrial hypothesis, and that most sightings could be explained by conventional means. The project was succeeded by Project Grudge in 1949 after internal disagreements arose regarding the nature and potential threat of UFOs, particularly within a classified 'Estimate of the Situation' report which allegedly supported the ETH. Skeptics also point to the lack of verifiable physical evidence and the subjective nature of witness testimony as major weaknesses in claims made by proponents of Project Sign.
subtitle: Early US Air Force UFO Investigation

claims.claim: Project Sign secretly concluded some UFOs were extraterrestrial.
claims.evidence: The 'Estimate of the Situation' report allegedly supported the extraterrestrial hypothesis (ETH).
claims.criticism: The 'Estimate of the Situation' was rejected by superiors and never officially endorsed. No definitive proof of extraterrestrial origin was found.

claims.claim: The Air Force suppressed evidence of UFOs being alien spacecraft.
claims.evidence: Proponents claim the project was prematurely shut down and replaced by more skeptical projects like Project Grudge.
claims.criticism: The Air Force states that the project was reorganized due to a need for more rigorous scientific investigation and a perceived lack of progress.

claims.claim: Unexplained sightings investigated by Project Sign remain compelling evidence of UFOs.
claims.evidence: Some reports lacked conventional explanations and involved credible witnesses.
claims.criticism: Many unexplained sightings are attributed to a lack of sufficient data, misidentification, or psychological factors. The absence of explanation does not automatically equate to extraterrestrial origin.

keyFigures.name: Captain Robert R. Ruppelt
keyFigures.role: Project Sign (and later Project Blue Book) director

keyFigures.name: Edward J. Ruppelt
keyFigures.role: Head of Project Blue Book, critical of early UFO interpretations

mythologyConnections: Modern mythology surrounding UFOs and aliens

timeline.event: Project Sign is initiated by the U.S. Air Force.
Initial investigations begin, collecting sighting reports.
The 'Estimate of the Situation' is allegedly drafted and rejected.
Project Sign is officially renamed and reorganized as Project Grudge.

sources.author: Edward J. Ruppelt
sources.work: The Report on Unidentified Flying Objects
sources.title: Edward J. Ruppelt

sources.author: Curtis Peebles
sources.work: Watch the Skies!: A Chronicle of the Flying Saucer Myth
sources.title: Curtis Peebles
